rows.v: Tennis term for a score of zero. Used throughout a game — '15-love', 'love-30', and so on.
She held to love, winning all four points without dropping a single one — a flawless service game.
Most likely from the phrase 'to play for love' (for nothing, no stakes); a popular but disputed theory traces it to the French 'l'œuf' (egg, for the zero's shape).
The score when both players reach 40-40 (three points each). From deuce, a player must win two points in a row to take the game.
The game reached deuce again and again, neither able to win the two straight points needed to close it out.
From the French 'à deux' (at two) — meaning two points are needed to win — via the Old French 'deus' (two).
The point won immediately after deuce. If the same player wins the next point, they take the game; if not, the score returns to deuce.
He won the deuce point for advantage, then sealed the game on the very next rally.
From the Old French 'avantage' (a favorable position) — the player who scores from deuce holds the upper hand, one point from the game.
The phrase announcing the end of a match: a 'game' is the first unit, a 'set' is a group of games, and 'match' is the whole contest, decided by sets.
With one final ace, the umpire called 'Game, set, and match' — the contest was over.
Each term nests inside the next: points build a game, games build a set, sets build the match — the tiered structure of tennis scoring stated in order.
A special game played at 6-6 in a set to decide the winner, first to 7 points (win by two), avoiding endlessly long sets.
Locked at 6-6, they went to a tiebreak, and she edged it 7-5 to take the set.
From the plain sense of 'breaking' a tie. Introduced in the 1970s (credited to Jimmy Van Alen) to keep sets from running on indefinitely.
A point that, if won by the returner, would win the game against the server. A pivotal moment, since holding serve is the norm.
She earned break point at 30-40 and ripped a return winner to steal his service game.
Descriptive: winning it 'breaks' the server's expected hold of their own game — among the highest-pressure points in tennis.
To 'hold' is to win your own service game; to 'break' is to win the game when your opponent is serving. The basic rhythm of a set.
Both players held serve comfortably until she finally earned the break to go up 5-4.
From 'holding' onto the advantage the server is expected to keep; 'break' from breaking that expected pattern of service dominance.
Slang for winning a set 6-0. The zero resembles a bagel. A 'double bagel' is a 6-0, 6-0 win.
She handed out a bagel in the first set, racing through it 6-0 before her opponent found any rhythm.
From the round, zero-shaped bagel standing in for the '0' in a 6-0 set — a piece of affectionate locker-room slang.
A stroke hit with the palm-side of the hand facing the direction of the swing — for most players, the more natural and powerful side.
He ran around his backhand to crush an inside-out forehand, his biggest weapon, into the open court.
Descriptive: the 'fore' (front) of the hand leads the stroke, as opposed to the back of the hand on a backhand.
A stroke hit with the back of the hand facing the swing's direction, played across the body. Can be one- or two-handed.
Her two-handed backhand down the line was a thing of beauty, flat and deep into the corner.
Descriptive: the 'back' of the hand leads the stroke. The one-handed backhand is the classic form; the two-hander rose to dominance in the modern game.
A shot struck before the ball bounces, usually played near the net to cut off the rally and finish the point quickly.
He charged the net and put away a crisp volley into the open court before she could recover.
From the French 'volée' (a flight), via 'voler' (to fly) — the ball is struck while still in flight, before touching the ground.
A shot hit after the ball has bounced once, from the back of the court. Forehands and backhands from the baseline are groundstrokes.
The two traded heavy groundstrokes from the baseline, neither giving an inch in the long exchange.
Descriptive: the ball is struck off the 'ground' after its bounce, distinguishing it from a volley taken out of the air.
A shot hit high over an opponent who is at the net, sending the ball deep behind them to reset or win the point.
Stranded at the net, he could only watch as her perfect lob floated over his head and dropped on the baseline.
From the old sense of 'lob' (to throw or hit in a high arc) — a soft, rising trajectory designed to clear the opponent.
A powerful overhead shot, hit like a serve, to put away a high ball — often the answer to a lob. Also called an 'overhead'.
She read the weak lob early and crushed an overhead smash that bounced into the stands.
From 'to smash' (to strike with great force). The overhead motion mirrors the serve and delivers one of the game's most forceful shots.
A delicately struck shot that barely clears the net and dies quickly, used to catch a deep opponent off guard.
With his rival pinned behind the baseline, he feathered a drop shot that two bounces couldn't save.
Descriptive: the ball 'drops' just over the net with heavy backspin and minimal pace — a finesse shot, the opposite of power.
A shot hit with backspin by drawing the racket down and through the ball, producing a low, skidding bounce. Common on the backhand.
She mixed in a low backhand slice that skidded and stayed low, disrupting his rhythm completely.
From the cutting, downward 'slicing' motion across the ball that imparts the backspin — the same root as the golf slice's glancing contact.
A serve that the receiver fails to touch at all, winning the point outright. The server's ultimate weapon.
Down break point, he fired an ace down the T at 130 mph to erase the danger instantly.
From the playing-card 'ace' (the single highest card) via its general sense of 'the best' — an unreturned serve being the perfect outcome.
A serve that fails — landing out, in the net, or with a foot fault. Each point allows two serve attempts before a fault costs the point.
His first serve clipped the net cord and dropped wide — a fault — leaving him a second serve to play.
From the general sense of 'fault' (an error or failing) — the serve fails to meet the rules, an unforced mistake on the most controlled shot.
Missing both serve attempts on a point, which hands the point directly to the opponent. A costly, self-inflicted error.
Serving for the set, she tightened up and double-faulted, gifting her opponent the crucial point.
Descriptive: two consecutive 'faults' (failed serves) in a row. Among the most demoralizing ways to lose a point in tennis.
A serve that clips the top of the net but still lands in the correct service box — it's replayed with no penalty. Also any point ordered to be replayed.
His serve ticked the net cord and dribbled in; the umpire called a let, and he served again.
From an archaic sense of 'let' meaning to hinder or obstruct (as in 'without let or hindrance') — the net 'lets' or interferes with the serve.
Each point gives the server two tries: the 'first serve' is hit harder and riskier; the safer 'second serve' must go in or it's a double fault.
He missed a big first serve, then spun in a cautious second serve to start the rally safely.
Descriptive of the two-attempt structure: the first is the aggressive bid, the second a controlled fallback to avoid the double fault.
A serve hit with heavy topspin that bounces high and jumps to the side, hard to attack. A reliable, safe second serve.
Her kick serve leapt up above his shoulder, forcing a weak return she pounced on.
From the way the heavy topspin makes the ball 'kick' upward and sideways off the bounce — a high, awkward bounce rather than a flat skid.
The line at the back of the court marking the boundary for play and the spot from which serves are struck. Baseliners play mostly from here.
A classic baseliner, she camped behind the baseline and dictated play with deep, heavy groundstrokes.
Descriptive: the 'base' line forming the rearmost boundary of each side of the court — the foundation line of the playing area.
The barrier dividing the court in half, which the ball must pass over. Also the act of 'coming to net' to volley.
He followed his approach to the net, looking to finish with a volley before she could pass him.
From the literal mesh net strung across the court's center — both the physical object and, by extension, the forecourt area beside it.
The two halves of each baseline: the 'deuce court' is the right side (where the deuce point is served), the 'ad court' the left (where advantage points are served).
On break point in the ad court, she served wide to pull him off the deuce side entirely.
Named for the score served to each side: 'deuce' points are played to the right court, 'advantage' (ad) points to the left.
The narrow lanes on each side of the court, in play only for doubles. Also called the 'tramlines'. A singles ball landing here is out.
His passing shot caught the doubles alley by an inch — in for doubles, but it would've been out in singles.
'Alley' from the narrow-lane sense; 'tramlines' (British) from the resemblance to the parallel rails of a tram track running down each side.
The marked rectangles into which a serve must land to be legal — one on each side, diagonally across from the server.
Her serve clipped the corner of the service box, perfectly placed and impossible to read.
Descriptive: the boxed area designated to receive the 'service' (serve). A serve missing the box is a fault.
An extended exchange of shots between players after the serve is returned, lasting until the point ends.
They engaged in a 30-shot rally, each chasing down impossible balls until she finally cracked a winner.
From the older sense of 'rally' (to come back together, regroup) — the back-and-forth gathering of shot after shot in a single point.
A shot hit while moving forward toward the net, designed to set up a winning volley by forcing a weak reply.
He drove a deep approach shot to her backhand and followed it in, ready to volley the floating return.
Descriptive: the shot played as the player 'approaches' the net, transitioning from baseline to forecourt to finish the point.
A mistake made entirely under the player's own control, not forced by a good shot from the opponent. A key stat for measuring sloppy play.
She lost the set on unforced errors, spraying easy balls long and wide rather than being beaten outright.
Descriptive: an error 'unforced' by the opponent's pressure — a self-inflicted miss, contrasted with a 'forced error' caused by a tough shot.
A shot the opponent can't touch, ending the point cleanly in the hitter's favor — the positive counterpart to an unforced error.
He ended the marathon rally with a forehand winner down the line, clean and untouchable.
From the plain sense of the shot that 'wins' the point outright — struck beyond the reach of the opponent.
To win the opponent's service game immediately after losing your own. A momentum-swinging recovery.
Right after dropping serve, she broke back to love, erasing the deficit and steadying the set.
Descriptive: regaining ('breaking back') the service break you just surrendered — answering a break with a break of your own.
Winning all four major championships (Australian, French, Wimbledon, US Open) in a single year. Also used loosely for the majors themselves.
Holding three majors, she chased the calendar Grand Slam at the US Open under enormous pressure.
Borrowed from the card game bridge (winning all the tricks) and earlier from golf, applied to sweeping tennis's four biggest titles.
The electronic ball-tracking system used to review whether a ball landed in or out. Players issue a 'challenge' to trigger a review.
He challenged the call, and Hawk-Eye showed the ball had clipped the line by a millimeter — point reversed.
Named for the brand of the tracking technology, evoking a hawk's sharp vision; the player's request to use it is called a 'challenge'.
A very high, slow, looping groundstroke that lands deep, used to disrupt an opponent's rhythm and buy time. Often a defensive or frustrating tactic.
Pushed off the court, she threw up a moonball to reset the point and scramble back into position.
A coinage evoking a ball lofted so high it seems to head for the moon — slow, towering, and deliberately rhythm-breaking.
